summary:
Karahan Tepe is a Pre-Pottery Neolithic archaeological site in the Tektek Mountains of Sanliurfa Province, southeastern Turkey, located approximately 46 kilometers east of [[event:3|Gobekli Tepe]]. Known to local inhabitants for generations but only beginning to receive systematic archaeological excavation in 2019, Karahan Tepe is increasingly recognized as potentially even larger and more significant than Gobekli Tepe, sharing its tradition of monumental T-shaped limestone pillar architecture.

Excavations at Karahan Tepe have uncovered a series of specially carved chambers cut into the bedrock and lined with T-shaped pillars bearing elaborate relief carvings. One chamber contains a remarkable series of phallus-headed human sculptures in relief on the pillars — a distinct and vivid iconographic program differing in content from Gobekli Tepe's animal reliefs while sharing the same architectural tradition. Another structure contains a striking life-size human head sculpture emerging from the bedrock floor. The site appears to date to roughly the same period as Gobekli Tepe (approximately 9600–8000 BCE) and forms part of a cluster of at least a dozen related monumental sites in the Tas Tepeler (Stone Hills) region of upper Mesopotamia.

The ongoing excavation of Karahan Tepe and related sites is rapidly transforming understanding of the cultural and religious landscape of the Pre-Pottery Neolithic in southeastern Anatolia, demonstrating that [[event:3|Gobekli Tepe]] was not an isolated phenomenon but one node in a dense network of ritual centers — a sacred landscape of considerable geographic extent — created by the hunter-gatherer and early farming communities of the region around 10,000–8,000 BCE.

The discovery and investigation of Karahan Tepe represents one of the most exciting developments in recent world archaeology, with the potential to fundamentally revise understanding of the scale, complexity, and ideological life of the world's earliest known ritual landscape.
